title: Vandesteene Breaks Through in Monaco Thriller as Cachinho's Red Flag Shakes the Principality
meta: Published: 25 May 2026 | By Elena Vasquez, Chief Race Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: Wesley Vandesteene celebrates his first victory of the 2026 season — a masterclass of patience and opportunism through the streets of Monte Carlo.
body:
The harbour glittered under the Monaco sun, the yachts bobbing gently in the afternoon light, but on the track above the water, chaos was brewing. When the chequered flag fell on the 2026 Monaco Grand Prix, it was Wesley Vandesteene who stood on the top step — the Mercedes driver having delivered a victory of patience, opportunism, and flawless execution. Behind him, the order of the championship had been scrambled once more. A red flag on lap 64 had erased the strategies, bunched the field, and set up a sprint to the finish that left the paddock breathless. And through it all, one voice on the radio — Robin Keisner's — captured the spirit of a race that refused to be tamed: "What a strategy guys and what a race, after Miami I thought our championship was over."

Vandesteene started fifth. He finished first. The gap to Jason Roy's Red Bull was 0.736 seconds — a margin that flattered the drama of the final stint. The Belgian had been patient in the early laps, conserving his tyres while Roy and the Ferraris scrapped ahead of him. When the safety car appeared on lap 16 for Julian Kondra's crash, Vandesteene was perfectly placed. When the red flag came on lap 64 — triggered by Afonso Cachinho's lock‑up into the chicane, collecting Richard Hicks and Eren Aygen in a three‑car pile‑up — Vandesteene held his nerve. And when the standing restart came, he launched off the line and took the lead from Roy into Sainte‑Devote, a move that would define his afternoon. He never gave it back.

The race had begun under the usual Monaco conditions — tight, tense, the walls close enough to touch. Jason Roy started from pole and led the early laps, but his advantage was never comfortable. Eetu Väisänen, starting second, reported mechanical issues on lap 12 — a drivability problem that made the car feel, in his words, "like driving on ice." He would fight through the afternoon, his Ferrari losing time in every sector, but his championship lead was slipping away with every lap. By the time the red flag fell, he was eighth, and Robin Keisner — who finished fourth — was closing the gap at the top of the standings to just four points.

The first major incident came on lap 16. Julian Kondra, the Alpine rookie who had qualified a stunning third, lost the rear of his car at the exit of the casino square and clipped the wall. The damage was terminal. Sarah Springfield, running close behind, also found the barriers moments later — her steering, she told her engineer, was "****** six ways from Sunday." The team called her in to retire. Two cars gone, and the safety car was deployed, triggering a wave of pit stops that would reshape the order.

The strategic gamble of the day belonged to those who stayed out during the safety car. Robin Keisner, Maikel Zwart, and Afonso Cachinho — against the advice of their engineers in some cases — kept their hard tyres and inherited track position. Keisner's radio crackled with disbelief when the red flag was announced: he had been on worn hards, hoping for a miracle, and the miracle arrived. When the field reset for the standing restart, he was fourth, his tyres fresh, his championship hopes revived. His engineer told him after the flag: "And that's P4." Keisner's reply was pure, unfiltered joy: "What a strategy guys and what a race, after Miami I thought our championship was over. I hope Aygen and Hicks are okay after that crash."

The crash itself was the most dramatic moment of the season so far. Afonso Cachinho, still chasing his first points of 2026, locked up under braking for the chicane after the tunnel. His Haas speared into the barrier, collecting Richard Hicks' Audi and Eren Aygen's Williams in the carnage. The circuit was blocked. The red flag flew. Three cars were eliminated. Cachinho's nightmare season — zero points, mounting pressure, and now a three‑car collision — plumbed new depths. Hicks and Aygen, both championship contenders, were left fuming on the sidelines, their races ended by a driver they had not even been racing.

When the standing restart came on lap 66, the race was a sprint. Vandesteene got the better launch, diving inside Roy into Sainte‑Devote and emerging in the lead. Roy, who had been the class of the field all afternoon, could not find a way back past. The Red Bull was quick, but Monaco is Monaco, and the walls do not yield. Behind them, Riley Swansson drove the race of his season to finish third — his first podium of 2026, and a result that will ease the pressure on the second Ferrari seat. Keisner held fourth, his recovery from 12th on the grid a reminder of his growing maturity. Lukas Wright, the 2024 champion, was fifth — another quiet but effective afternoon for the Racing Bulls driver, who had made three identical overtakes at the same corner earlier in the race, each one a carbon copy of the last, each one executed with the precision of a driver who has found his rhythm.

Further back, Sam Fact continued his remarkable resurgence. A podium in Canada, and now sixth in Monaco — the Aston Martin driver is no longer the story of a man searching for form, but of a driver who has found it. Maikel Zwart finished seventh, though his radio crackled with the usual frustrations: "Guys, problems again, it's getting worse now." His engineer's reply — "Copy, we will look into it" — was the hollow reassurance of a team that has run out of answers. Eetu Väisänen limped home eighth, his championship lead reduced to four points over Keisner. Joshua Nathanial recovered from his early spin to finish ninth, and Philippe Verhaeren took the final point in tenth.

The championship has tightened once more. Väisänen still leads, but Keisner is just four points behind. Eren Aygen, who failed to score, drops to third. Vandesteene's victory vaults him to fourth in the standings, and Jason Roy — second in the race, sixth in the championship — is looming. The constructors' table is equally tight: Ferrari lead with 152 points, but Williams are level on points, and Audi are just one behind on 151. McLaren, Red Bull, and Mercedes are all within striking distance. Eight races in, and the 2026 season has already delivered seven different winners. The only certainty is that nothing is certain.

The paddock packs up in the Monaco dusk, the yachts still bobbing in the harbour. The circus heads now to Barcelona, where the heat will be different, the corners faster, and the championship fight even tighter. Vandesteene has his first win of the season. Keisner has his redemption. Cachinho has another weekend to forget. And the championship, as always, waits for no one.
